This time, the regulated on-ramps — spot Bitcoin ETFs, prime brokerage services, custodial platforms, and compliance frameworks — that major financial institutions spent billions constructing remain operational and intact. The plumbing, in other words, has survived even as the asset it was built to carry has cratered.

For Bitcoin bulls, this resilience represents a critical distinction. Previous bear markets destroyed both price and infrastructure simultaneously, forcing the industry to rebuild from scratch each cycle. The persistence of institutional participation suggests that the current decline is a repricing event rather than an existential crisis for the asset class. Wall Street firms that launched crypto trading desks, asset managers running Bitcoin ETFs, and banks offering digital asset custody have shown no signs of retreating from the market.

The selloff's severity, however, should not be understated. A near-50 percent decline tests the conviction of even the most committed holders, and the speed of the drawdown has triggered significant liquidations across leveraged positions. Retail sentiment has soured considerably, with on-chain data indicating capitulation among shorter-term holders.

The central question now facing the market is whether durable infrastructure translates into durable demand. The bull case rests on the argument that institutional rails make recovery faster and more robust — that when capital eventually rotates back into risk assets, the pipes are already in place to absorb it. The bear case counters that infrastructure alone cannot arrest a downturn driven by macroeconomic headwinds, regulatory uncertainty, or simply diminished appetite for speculative assets. What is clear is that this cycle is structurally different from its predecessors, and how Bitcoin emerges from this drawdown will say as much about the maturation of crypto markets as any rally ever did.

articles.headline: Quantum Threat to RSA Encryption Drops to 100,000 Qubits
articles.subheadline: Australian researchers show common encryption could fall to a quantum computer within the decade, though engineering hurdles remain formidable
articles.body:
A team of researchers has demonstrated that breaking RSA encryption — the backbone of online banking, secure messaging, and government communications — could require as few as 98,000 quantum bits, a tenfold reduction from previous estimates and a threshold that some quantum computing firms aim to reach before 2035.

The study, led by Paul Webster at Australia-based Iceberg Quantum, builds on a trajectory of rapidly shrinking estimates. In 2019, Google Quantum AI researcher Craig Gidney co-authored work that cut the required qubit count from 170 million to 20 million. By 2025, Gidney had pushed it below one million. Now, Webster's team has driven it down to roughly 100,000 by leveraging a qubit connectivity scheme known as qLDPC (quantum low-density parity-check) code, which allows qubits to interact with distant partners rather than only their nearest neighbors. This increased connectivity effectively compresses the information density of a quantum processor, making each qubit work harder.

According to the team's calculations, published as a preprint on arXiv, approximately 98,000 superconducting qubits of the type currently manufactured by IBM and Google could crack a standard RSA key in about a month of continuous computation. Doing so in a single day would require around 471,000 qubits. The estimate is largely hardware-agnostic, depending primarily on error rates and processing speed rather than the specific qubit technology employed.

Not everyone is sounding the alarm just yet. Gidney cautioned that the engineering demands of implementing long-range qubit connections make the hardware "harder to make, and making the hardware is already the hardest part." Scott Aaronson of the University of Texas at Austin expressed similar reservations on his blog, pointing to the practical difficulties of wiring distant qubits together. IBM, which has championed qLDPC codes and adapted its hardware to support them, called the approach a future "cornerstone" of its quantum architecture but declined to comment on whether the new scheme could be realized in practice. Alternative platforms using trapped ions or cold atoms can more easily implement distant qubit connections, but their slower operational speeds push the required qubit count back into the millions.

The findings underscore the urgency of the global transition to post-quantum cryptography standards, which the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology finalized in 2024. Lawrence Cohen, a co-author at Iceberg Quantum, argued that conservative timelines are dangerous when the stakes include access to encrypted emails, financial systems, and classified government files. "Somebody breaking RSA would have big consequences," he said, adding that RSA factoring also serves as a valuable benchmark for quantum computing progress — and that the same techniques could accelerate useful simulations in quantum chemistry and materials science.

articles.headline: Anthropic Abandons Key Safety Pledge as AI Competition Intensifies
articles.subheadline: The startup once known as the 'responsible' AI lab will no longer delay risky development if rivals are close behind
articles.body:
Anthropic, the artificial intelligence company long regarded as the industry's most safety-conscious major player, has quietly dismantled one of its foundational commitments — announcing it will no longer pause potentially dangerous AI development if it believes a competitor is close to matching its capabilities.

The policy change, disclosed in a Tuesday blog post updating Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy, marks one of the most significant reversals in the brief but turbulent history of the AI industry. When the San Francisco-based company first published the policy in 2023, it explicitly pledged to delay development of AI systems that might pose serious risks. The revised language introduces a critical exception: Anthropic will not halt progress if it determines it lacks a meaningful lead over its rivals. In practical terms, the company has subordinated its safety commitments to competitive positioning — precisely the dynamic its founders once warned could prove catastrophic.

The shift is especially striking given Anthropic's origin story. The company was founded in 2021 by Dario and Daniela Amodei, former OpenAI executives who departed in part over concerns about the pace of AI development outrunning safety research. Anthropic raised billions of dollars from investors including Google and Salesforce on the strength of its reputation as the responsible alternative in a field increasingly defined by breakneck speed and winner-take-all economics. Its Claude family of AI models was marketed not just on capability but on trustworthiness.

The revision reflects a broader transformation across the AI sector, where startups that once emphasized caution and alignment research are pivoting toward commercial viability and market share. With OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Meta, and a growing cohort of well-funded competitors racing to develop ever more powerful systems, the economic pressure to keep pace has evidently overwhelmed Anthropic's institutional commitment to restraint. The company appears to have concluded that unilateral caution is an untenable strategy in a multi-player race.

Critics are likely to seize on the announcement as evidence that voluntary safety pledges in the AI industry carry little long-term weight, potentially strengthening the case for binding government regulation. If the company most publicly identified with responsible development cannot sustain its own guardrails under competitive pressure, the question of who — if anyone — will slow the race becomes considerably more urgent.

articles.headline: CISA Gutted: US Cyber Agency Loses a Third of Staff Under Trump
articles.subheadline: Bipartisan lawmakers warn the nation's lead cybersecurity agency is unprepared for a crisis as government shutdown compounds deep cuts
articles.body:
The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, the federal government's frontline defender against digital threats, has lost roughly one-third of its workforce over the past year under Trump administration cuts and layoffs, leaving it badly weakened at a moment of escalating cyber risk, according to bipartisan lawmakers and industry leaders.

The damage, detailed in a Cyberscoop investigation drawing on sources across Congress and the private sector, extends beyond headcount. CISA's counter-ransomware initiative has been dismantled, its secure software development programs curtailed, and key members of its election security team — the unit responsible for safeguarding the integrity of U.S. elections — were let go last year. Critics say the administration's fixation on relitigating false claims about the 2020 election has driven a deliberate deprioritization of the agency's core mission.

The situation has grown more acute since a partial federal government shutdown began on February 14, triggered by a congressional standoff over immigration funding following the killing of two U.S. citizens by federal agents. CISA is now reportedly operating at just 38 percent of normal staffing levels. Hundreds of additional personnel had already been reassigned to support other Department of Homeland Security agencies as part of the administration's immigration enforcement expansion, further draining the cyber agency's capacity.

Leadership has also come under scrutiny. CISA has been without a Senate-confirmed permanent director since President Trump took office in January 2025. Acting director Madhu Gottumukkala, who has led the agency in the interim, has drawn criticism from multiple sources for struggling to steer the organization and allegedly creating operational security issues. In a statement to TechCrunch, Gottumukkala said CISA "remains unwavering in its commitment to protect our federal networks from malicious cyber threat actors despite the multi-week government shutdown."

The erosion of CISA's capabilities arrives against a backdrop of intensifying threats. State-sponsored intrusions from China, Russia, and other adversaries continue to target U.S. critical infrastructure, while ransomware attacks on hospitals, schools, and municipal governments show no signs of abating. Former officials and cybersecurity professionals warn that the hollowing out of the agency responsible for coordinating the nation's cyber defense leaves the United States significantly more vulnerable — and that rebuilding the lost expertise will take years, even if funding and political will materialize.

articles.headline: Nvidia's $78 Billion Sales Forecast Lifts Stocks After Hours
articles.subheadline: Chipmaker's fiscal Q1 guidance beats estimates by more than $5 billion, reigniting AI trade confidence
articles.body:
Nvidia Corp. delivered a fiscal first-quarter sales forecast of approximately $78 billion on Tuesday, handily surpassing Wall Street's average estimate of $72.8 billion and sending its shares up roughly 1.5% in extended trading. The blockbuster guidance renewed investor enthusiasm for the artificial intelligence trade that has been the dominant force behind the broader equity rally.

U.S. stocks climbed in late trading following the announcement, with a $700 billion exchange-traded fund tracking the S&P 500 rising after the close of regular hours. The results underscored Nvidia's continued grip on the AI infrastructure buildout, reinforcing its status as the most closely watched barometer for demand in the revolutionary technology sector. The more than $5 billion beat over consensus expectations suggests that hyperscale cloud providers and enterprise customers are accelerating, not moderating, their capital expenditure on AI computing.

The strong outlook arrives at a moment when markets had begun to question the sustainability of AI-linked valuations. Concerns over stretched multiples and the possibility of a spending plateau had weighed on sentiment in recent weeks. Nvidia's numbers effectively pushed back against that narrative, offering concrete evidence that the demand trajectory for advanced AI chips remains steep.

Citi Wealth's Kate Moore signaled continued bullishness on equities, telling Bloomberg she remains "still all-in" on stocks — a view that Nvidia's results may now embolden among a broader cohort of institutional investors. The chipmaker's guidance is likely to set the tone for technology trading in the sessions ahead, with downstream effects on semiconductor suppliers, data center operators, and the broader AI ecosystem.

For markets, the after-hours move suggests Wednesday's open could bring a meaningful rotation back into AI-linked names. Investors will be parsing Nvidia's full earnings commentary for signals on supply chain dynamics, product cycle timing, and geographic demand patterns — all factors that could determine whether the AI rally enters its next leg higher or meets renewed resistance.

articles.headline: Tether Pours $200M Into Digital Marketplace Whop at $1.6B Valuation
articles.subheadline: The stablecoin giant aims to embed USDT payments into a creator economy platform serving 18.4 million users
articles.body:
Tether, the company behind the world's largest stablecoin USDT, is making its biggest push yet into consumer-facing commerce with a $200 million investment in Whop, a digital marketplace for creators and software tools. The deal values Whop at $1.6 billion, according to CEO Steven Schwartz.

Whop operates as an online hub where creators sell access to software, trading communities, educational courses, and other digital products. The platform claims 18.4 million users and roughly $3 billion in annual creator earnings, with gross transaction volume growing approximately 25% month over month. Under the terms of the investment, Whop will integrate Tether's cryptocurrency wallet infrastructure, enabling users and sellers to hold, transact, and settle payments using USDT and Tether's newer U.S.-focused USAT token — bypassing traditional banks and card networks entirely.

The strategic logic for Tether is clear: push its $185 billion stablecoin ecosystem beyond trading desks and into the daily financial lives of ordinary internet users. USDT has already established itself as a primary vehicle for accessing U.S. dollar liquidity in emerging markets, and embedding it natively within a fast-growing creator marketplace could accelerate that adoption curve. The funding will specifically support Whop's expansion into Latin America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific — regions where dollar-denominated stablecoins often serve as a practical alternative to volatile local currencies or limited banking infrastructure.

The deal also signals Tether's ambition to move up the financial services stack. Beyond simple payments, the partnership envisions adding lending and borrowing tools powered by decentralized finance infrastructure, potentially turning Whop into a more comprehensive financial platform for its creator base.

The investment comes at a moment of intensifying competition in the stablecoin sector. Circle, issuer of rival stablecoin USDC, recently reported fourth-quarter earnings that beat analyst estimates, with its shares surging on growing issuance. For Tether, locking in distribution through platforms like Whop represents a defensive as much as offensive strategy — ensuring USDT remains the default digital dollar as stablecoins increasingly penetrate mainstream commerce.

articles.headline: Wall Street Rallies as Investors Position Ahead of Nvidia Earnings
articles.subheadline: Major indexes climbed on February 25 as markets braced for the AI chipmaker's closely watched quarterly results
articles.body:
U.S. equity markets closed sharply higher on Tuesday as investors positioned themselves ahead of Nvidia's highly anticipated earnings report, with broad-based buying lifting major indexes in a session marked by renewed risk appetite.

The rally reflected the outsized influence Nvidia continues to exert over market sentiment. As the world's most valuable chipmaker and a bellwether for artificial intelligence spending, the company's quarterly results have become one of the most consequential single-stock events on Wall Street's calendar. Traders appeared willing to bet that the report would deliver another round of blowout numbers, bidding up shares across the technology sector and beyond.

The advance came amid a market environment that has been increasingly shaped by the AI investment cycle. Nvidia's earnings have repeatedly served as inflection points for broader market direction, with the company's guidance on data center demand and chip supply offering a real-time read on the pace of AI infrastructure buildout by hyperscale cloud providers and enterprise customers. Investors have been particularly focused on whether the momentum behind Nvidia's next-generation GPU architectures can sustain the company's extraordinary growth trajectory.

Beyond the Nvidia catalyst, the session's broad rally suggested underlying confidence in the economic backdrop, even as markets continue to navigate uncertainty around Federal Reserve policy and geopolitical risks. The willingness of investors to add exposure ahead of a potentially volatile earnings event pointed to a market that remains fundamentally constructive, with dip-buying behavior and momentum-chasing strategies continuing to support prices.

With Nvidia set to report after the bell, the stakes for the post-earnings session were significant. A strong beat could extend the rally and reinforce the AI-driven bull case that has powered much of the market's gains, while any disappointment in revenue, margins, or forward guidance risked triggering a sharp reversal — not just in Nvidia shares, but across the constellation of semiconductor, cloud, and AI-adjacent stocks that have moved in sympathy with the chipmaker's fortunes.

articles.headline: Fox Corp. Hit With Rare Double Downgrade as NFL Renewal Costs Loom
articles.subheadline: Bank of America cuts Fox to underperform, warning that the league's surging popularity could drive up rights fees
articles.body:
Bank of America has issued a rare double downgrade of Fox Corp. shares, cutting its rating from buy to underperform over concerns that the National Football League's growing dominance of U.S. television audiences could significantly inflate the cost of renewing broadcast rights.

The move, reported on February 25, makes BofA the lone bear among analysts covering the media company. The central thesis is counterintuitive but straightforward: the NFL's expanding cultural and commercial grip on American viewers strengthens the league's hand at the bargaining table, potentially forcing Fox to pay substantially more when its current rights deal comes up for renewal. For a company that has built much of its programming identity around professional football, walking away is not a realistic option — a dynamic the NFL understands well.

Fox Corp. has long depended on NFL broadcasts as a cornerstone of its linear television strategy. Live sports remain one of the few categories of programming that reliably delivers large, real-time audiences in an era of streaming fragmentation. That scarcity value, however, cuts both ways. While NFL games generate premium advertising revenue and serve as a promotional platform for the rest of Fox's schedule, the cost of securing those games has escalated with each successive negotiation cycle. The league's most recent round of media deals, finalized in 2021, was valued at roughly $113 billion over 11 years across multiple networks.

Bank of America's downgrade reflects a broader anxiety in the media sector about the rising price of must-have sports content. Companies from Amazon to Netflix have entered the bidding landscape for live sports rights, intensifying competition and driving valuations higher. For legacy broadcasters like Fox, which lack the diversified revenue streams of big tech platforms, each incremental dollar spent on rights fees carries outsized risk to margins.

The double downgrade — skipping the intermediate "neutral" rating entirely — signals a high degree of conviction in the bearish case. Investors will now be watching closely for any signals from Fox management about its negotiating posture and willingness to absorb higher costs to retain its NFL portfolio.

articles.headline: EQT Predicts Data Center Deals Will Surpass $100 Billion
articles.subheadline: Private equity firm's partner highlights surging AI infrastructure demand as industry forecasts $3 trillion in total investment
articles.body:
Data center dealmaking is poised to exceed $100 billion as artificial intelligence infrastructure demand accelerates at an unprecedented pace, according to Jan Vesely, a partner at global private equity firm EQT.

Speaking with Bloomberg's Dani Burger, Vesely outlined a bullish outlook for the sector, underscoring how the rapid buildout of AI capabilities is reshaping capital flows across the technology infrastructure landscape. The projection comes as estimates from Morgan Stanley and Moody's Ratings suggest that cumulative data center investment — including the construction of facilities and their associated power supplies — will need to surpass $3 trillion to meet anticipated demand.

The staggering figures reflect a fundamental shift in how the global economy is being rewired around AI workloads. Training and running large language models, powering enterprise AI applications, and supporting the broader generative AI ecosystem all require vast amounts of computing power housed in energy-intensive data centers. That demand has turned what was once a niche real estate category into one of the hottest asset classes in private equity, infrastructure investing, and corporate capital expenditure planning.

EQT, headquartered in Stockholm and managing over $130 billion in assets, has been among the private equity firms most aggressively positioning themselves in the digital infrastructure space. The firm's outlook suggests that both strategic acquirers and financial sponsors will continue competing for data center assets, driving transaction volumes to levels that would have been difficult to imagine even two years ago. Power availability, permitting timelines, and access to renewable energy sources have emerged as key differentiators in deal negotiations, adding complexity — and premium pricing — to an already competitive market.

The forecasts signal that the AI infrastructure boom is far from peaking. With hyperscale cloud providers, sovereign wealth funds, and private capital all vying for a share of the buildout, the data center sector appears set to remain at the center of one of the largest capital deployment cycles in recent memory.

articles.headline: CME Hit by Second Major Trading Glitch in a Month, Halting Gas and Metals
articles.subheadline: Natural gas futures were disrupted for over 30 minutes Wednesday, raising reliability concerns at the world's largest derivatives exchange
articles.body:
CME Group, the world's largest derivatives exchange, was forced to halt trading in U.S. natural gas and metals futures on Wednesday after a technical glitch disrupted its Globex electronic trading platform — the second significant outage to hit heavily traded commodity markets in roughly a month.

Natural gas futures, among the most actively traded commodity contracts globally, were offline for more than 30 minutes before CME restored operations. Metals contracts on the exchange were also affected by the disruption, though the full scope of impacted products was not immediately clear. CME did not provide detailed information about the root cause of the technical failure.

The incident is particularly notable given its proximity to a similar disruption just weeks earlier, raising questions about the resilience of critical market infrastructure that underpins trillions of dollars in annual trading volume. CME's Globex platform serves as the backbone for futures and options trading across energy, metals, agricultural commodities, interest rates, and equity indexes, making even brief outages consequential for traders managing real-time risk.

Repeated disruptions at major exchanges tend to draw scrutiny from regulators and market participants alike. The Commodity Futures Trading Commission oversees CME's operations and has historically pushed for robust system safeguards at designated contract markets. While a 30-minute halt may appear minor in isolation, back-to-back incidents can erode confidence among institutional traders and algorithmic market makers who depend on uninterrupted access to manage positions and hedge exposure.

Natural gas markets have been especially volatile in recent months, amplifying the stakes of any trading interruption. A halt during periods of high volatility can trap participants in positions they cannot adjust, potentially magnifying losses or forcing disorderly trading once markets reopen. CME confirmed that trading had resumed following the Wednesday disruption, but the exchange faces mounting pressure to explain the recurring technical issues and outline steps to prevent further incidents.

articles.headline: Ex-L3Harris Executive Sentenced to 87 Months for Selling Zero-Day Exploits to Russia
articles.subheadline: Peter Williams sold stolen hacking tools to sanctioned Russian broker Operation Zero for $1.3 million in cryptocurrency
articles.body:
A former executive at U.S. defense contractor L3Harris has been sentenced to more than seven years in federal prison after admitting to stealing advanced hacking tools and selling them to a Russian exploit broker during the ongoing war in Ukraine. The case represents one of the most significant leaks of Western-developed offensive cyber capabilities in recent memory.

Peter Williams, a 39-year-old Australian citizen who lived in Washington, D.C., served as general manager of Trenchant — L3Harris's secretive division responsible for developing zero-day exploits and surveillance tools for the U.S. government and allied intelligence agencies. Between 2022 and 2025, prosecutors say Williams exploited his unrestricted access to company networks, downloading hacking tools onto a portable hard drive and later to a personal computer. He then contacted Operation Zero, a Russian exploit broker that the U.S. government has labeled "one of the world's most nefarious," using a pseudonym. In exchange, Williams received $1.3 million in cryptocurrency, which he spent on a house, jewelry, and luxury watches.

The Department of Justice alleged the stolen tools could have enabled access to "millions of computers and devices around the world." While the specific exploits have not been publicly identified, court proceedings and Operation Zero's own public statements suggest the tools likely targeted widely used consumer platforms including Apple's iOS, Google's Android, and popular web browsers. Trenchant estimated the value of the stolen intellectual property at $35 million, though noted the tools were not formally classified as government secrets. Evidence presented in court indicated that at least some of the stolen code had already been resold — prosecutors noted that Williams himself recognized code he had written appearing in the hands of a South Korean broker.

On the same day as Williams's sentencing, the U.S. Treasury Department imposed sanctions on Operation Zero and its founder, Sergey Zelenyuk, confirming for the first time that Williams's buyer was the Russian firm. Treasury also revealed that Operation Zero had sold the stolen tools to "at least one unauthorized user," though the identity of that end user remains unknown. The Treasury simultaneously sanctioned an alleged member of the Trickbot cybercrime gang who reportedly worked with Operation Zero, raising the possibility that the exploits may have reached criminal organizations in addition to state actors.

Williams's background made the betrayal particularly striking. Before joining L3Harris, he had served in Australia's military and worked at the country's premier foreign intelligence agency, building a reputation as an accomplished hacker. His fall underscores the acute insider threat facing the offensive cyber industry, where a small number of individuals hold access to tools worth millions on the global exploit market. Critical questions remain unanswered, including whether Apple, Google, or other affected technology companies have been notified about the compromised zero-day vulnerabilities so they can issue patches to protect their users.

articles.headline: Nvidia Earnings Arrive as Jittery Market Tests AI Conviction
articles.subheadline: Wednesday's after-hours report lands amid rising investor anxiety over the durability of the AI spending boom
articles.body:
Nvidia Corp. is set to deliver its latest quarterly results Wednesday afternoon, a report that carries outsized significance for a U.S. stock market deeply tethered to the artificial intelligence narrative. The chipmaker's earnings land at a moment when investor patience for any stumble in the AI growth story appears to be running thin.

As the dominant supplier of the high-performance GPUs powering AI training and inference workloads, Nvidia has become the single most consequential earnings event on Wall Street's calendar. The company's results serve as a de facto referendum on whether the hundreds of billions of dollars flowing into AI infrastructure from hyperscale cloud providers and enterprises are translating into sustained demand — or beginning to plateau.

The stakes are elevated this quarter by a broader shift in market sentiment. After a prolonged rally driven largely by AI enthusiasm, investors have grown increasingly skittish about valuations and the timeline for AI investments to generate meaningful returns. Any sign of slowing order growth, softer forward guidance, or margin compression could reverberate well beyond Nvidia's own stock, dragging the semiconductor sector and major indices along with it.

Analysts will be scrutinizing several key metrics: revenue from the data center segment, which has been the engine of Nvidia's explosive growth; the trajectory of demand for its latest-generation chips; and management's commentary on supply constraints and competitive dynamics. Commentary on China-related export restrictions and their impact on sales will also be closely watched, as geopolitical tensions continue to shape the semiconductor landscape.

For the broader market, Wednesday's report represents a binary event. A strong beat with robust guidance could reignite confidence in the AI trade and provide a floor for technology stocks. A miss — or even guidance that merely meets expectations — risks accelerating the rotation away from AI-linked names that has been gathering momentum in recent weeks. In a market with no appetite for ambiguity, Nvidia's numbers will speak volumes about where the AI investment cycle stands.

articles.headline: Data Center Backlash Intensifies as States Weigh Construction Bans
articles.subheadline: From New York to New Orleans, a growing wave of moratoriums threatens Big Tech's $650 billion infrastructure buildout
articles.body:
A rising tide of public opposition to data center construction is sweeping across the United States, with communities and state legislatures moving to pause or restrict the massive infrastructure buildout fueling the artificial intelligence boom.

New York became the latest flashpoint earlier this month when State Senator Liz Krueger and Assemblymember Anna Kelles introduced legislation that would impose a three-year moratorium on new data center permits statewide — what the lawmakers called the "strongest" such proposal in the country. The bill would buy time for regulators to assess the environmental and economic toll the facilities are exacting on local communities. New York joins a rapidly expanding list of jurisdictions pumping the brakes: New Orleans passed a one-year construction moratorium in recent weeks, Madison, Wisconsin enacted a similar freeze in January, and numerous communities across Georgia and Michigan have adopted comparable restrictions.

The opposition spans the political spectrum. In Florida, Governor Ron DeSantis unveiled an AI "bill of rights" empowering communities to limit data center development. Vermont Senator Bernie Sanders has called for a nationwide moratorium. Arizona Governor Katie Hobbs has endorsed stripping the industry of tax incentives. The backlash is fueled by mounting concerns over energy affordability, water consumption, noise pollution, and strain on local infrastructure. In New York, 30 state lawmakers recently urged Governor Kathy Hochul to declare an "energy state of emergency" amid soaring utility rates — a crisis that data center growth is widely seen as worsening.

The political headwinds arrive at a precarious moment for the technology sector. Amazon, Google, Meta, and Microsoft collectively plan to spend approximately $650 billion in capital expenditures over the next year, with the overwhelming majority directed toward data center expansion. Yet polling suggests that momentum is shifting against them: an Echelon Insights survey found 46 percent of respondents would oppose a data center being built in their community, compared with just 35 percent in favor. A separate Politico poll indicated that many voters remain undecided, leaving public sentiment up for grabs.

Tech companies are responding on multiple fronts. A planned "Rate Payer Protection Pledge" would make companies responsible for supplying power to new AI facilities, and several firms — including Microsoft, Google, and Meta — have committed to paying for additions to local electrical grids. Some are going further, constructing private power sources in what has been described as a "shadow grid" strategy. But that approach has already generated controversy: Elon Musk's xAI built methane gas turbines at its Memphis data center that the Environmental Protection Agency ruled were operating illegally without air quality permits, prompting environmental groups to announce plans to sue. The industry is also mobilizing a lobbying campaign, with major operators reportedly planning a "blitz" of targeted advertising and community engagement in regions where they build.

Dan Diorio of the Data Center Coalition argued that the facilities can be a boon to smaller communities, providing tax revenue without straining limited resources. But with more than 40 states offering some form of tax break for data centers, critics increasingly question whether the public is getting a fair deal — setting the stage for a prolonged fight over how and where the infrastructure powering AI's future will be built.

articles.headline: Digital Twin and Bayesian Optimization Slash Particle Accelerator Tuning Time
articles.subheadline: Researchers demonstrate a hybrid approach that cuts tuning iterations by up to sixfold while achieving near-perfect beam transmission
articles.body:
A team of physicists has developed a two-stage tuning strategy for particle accelerators that pairs a computational digital twin with Bayesian optimization, dramatically reducing the time needed to achieve high-quality beam transmission. The work, carried out at the ISAC post-accelerator facility, was detailed in a paper published on the arXiv preprint server on February 23.

The approach, developed by O. Hassan, O. Shelbaya, P. M. Jung, O. Kester, T. Planche, and W. Fedorko, tackles a persistent operational challenge in accelerator physics: the complex, high-dimensional problem of simultaneously tuning beam optics and steering parameters. Rather than treating both tasks as a single optimization problem, the researchers split them apart. A digital twin model first pre-sets the machine's optical configuration, computing end-to-end tunes in a matter of seconds. Bayesian optimization then handles the remaining steering adjustments during live beam operation, all while respecting device bounds, slew-rate limits, and loss interlocks designed to protect the machine.

The results across three experimental case studies were striking. By decoupling optics from steering, the team reduced the number of iterations required to reach high-transmission tunes by a factor of four to six compared to a fully Bayesian baseline that attempted to optimize both optics and steering simultaneously. Final beam transmissions consistently landed in the mid- to high-90 percent range — a benchmark that represents near-optimal performance for such facilities.

Beyond raw speed, the factorized approach offers several practical advantages for day-to-day accelerator operations. Reducing the dimensionality of the parameter space makes convergence more predictable, a crucial property when expensive beam time is at stake. It also simplifies the enforcement of operational safeguards, since the digital twin can be constrained independently of the online optimization loop. The model's direct interface with the facility's control system means the strategy is not merely a theoretical exercise but a deployable operational tool.

The work reflects a broader trend in accelerator physics toward integrating machine learning and model-based techniques into real-time facility operations. As accelerator facilities worldwide push for higher intensities, tighter tolerances, and more efficient use of beam time, hybrid strategies that leverage both physics-based models and data-driven optimization are likely to become standard practice. The ISAC results suggest that the most effective path forward may not be fully autonomous AI-driven tuning, but rather a carefully structured collaboration between computational models and intelligent search algorithms.

articles.headline: Google Brings AI-Powered Task Automation to Android via Gemini
articles.subheadline: New beta feature lets Gemini order rides, food, and groceries autonomously — but only on select devices and in limited markets
articles.body:
Google announced on Wednesday a suite of updates to its Gemini AI assistant on Android, headlined by a new capability that allows the AI to execute multi-step tasks such as ordering an Uber or placing a food delivery — effectively turning the assistant into an autonomous agent that can navigate apps on a user's behalf.

The automation feature, launching in beta, is initially restricted to apps in the food, grocery, and rideshare categories, and will only be available on the Pixel 10, Pixel 10 Pro, and Samsung Galaxy S26 series devices. Geographic availability is limited to the United States and South Korea at launch. Google has built in several safeguards: automations require an explicit voice or text command to initiate, users can monitor progress in real time and halt any task mid-execution, and the operations run inside a secure virtual window that is sandboxed from the rest of the device's data.

The release positions Google squarely in an intensifying race among major AI companies to build consumer-facing agents that go beyond answering questions to actually completing tasks. OpenAI's ChatGPT already offers scheduled tasks and a computer-use agent capable of navigating calendars and generating presentations. Anthropic's Cowork feature extends its Claude model to non-coding file and task management. A viral newcomer called OpenClaw has also gained traction for handling everyday chores like email, calendar management, and flight check-ins. Google's approach differs by embedding automation directly into the mobile operating system layer, potentially giving it deeper integration with the Android app ecosystem.

Alongside the automation rollout, Google expanded its Gemini-powered scam detection for phone calls to the Samsung Galaxy S26 series in the U.S., complementing existing availability on Pixel devices across six countries. The company is also deploying on-device scam text detection on Pixel 10 devices in the U.S., Canada, and the U.K., with Galaxy S26 support coming soon. Additionally, Google's Circle to Search feature — which lets users draw gestures on their screen to initiate visual searches — now supports identifying all items visible on the display simultaneously, rather than requiring users to select objects one at a time.

The timing of Google's announcement underscores a widening gap between Android and iOS in the AI assistant race. Apple has repeatedly delayed the launch of its overhauled, AI-powered Siri, most recently pushing the release further into 2026. Google, by contrast, has maintained a steady cadence of Gemini enhancements delivered through operating system updates and its periodic Pixel Drop releases, steadily expanding the assistant's capabilities from conversational tool to autonomous agent.

articles.headline: Tikehau Capital Co-Founder Addresses Rising Private Credit Fears
articles.subheadline: Mounting default warnings and AI-driven disruption concerns rattle investors in the $1.7 trillion private credit market
articles.body:
Tikehau Capital co-founder Mathieu Chabran has waded into the growing debate over risks building in the private credit market, as investors grapple with warnings about rising defaults and the potential for artificial intelligence to reshape the lending landscape.

Speaking on Bloomberg's Open Interest program on February 25, Chabran addressed mounting anxiety among market participants who have watched private credit swell into one of the most prominent corners of alternative finance. The sector, which has ballooned past $1.7 trillion in assets globally, has drawn increasing scrutiny from regulators, ratings agencies, and institutional investors concerned that years of rapid growth may have sown the seeds of future credit losses. With interest rates remaining elevated and economic uncertainty persisting in key markets, the specter of rising defaults has moved from a theoretical worry to a more pressing concern for allocators with heavy exposure to the asset class.

The Paris-based Tikehau Capital, which manages approximately €47 billion in assets across private credit, private equity, real assets, and capital markets strategies, occupies a significant position in European alternative investment management. Chabran's comments come at a moment when the private credit industry faces a confluence of pressures: borrowers who locked in floating-rate loans during the low-rate era are now shouldering substantially higher debt-servicing costs, while the rapid advancement of AI technologies threatens to disrupt business models across the portfolios of direct lenders.

The concern over AI-driven disruption adds a newer dimension to the private credit risk conversation. As artificial intelligence tools accelerate automation and reshape competitive dynamics across industries, lenders face the challenge of underwriting credits in an environment where the long-term viability of borrowers may be harder to assess. Companies that once appeared to occupy defensible market positions could find their revenue streams eroded faster than traditional credit analysis would anticipate.

The broader private credit industry has faced a drumbeat of cautionary voices in recent months, with several prominent asset managers and central bank officials flagging the opacity of the market, the concentration of risk among non-bank lenders, and the potential for valuation marks to lag underlying deterioration in credit quality. For firms like Tikehau, which have built their franchises on the promise of superior risk-adjusted returns through private lending, navigating this environment will require demonstrating that disciplined underwriting can withstand the stresses that critics have long warned about.

articles.headline: Trump Administration Directs Diplomats to Fight Foreign Data Laws
articles.subheadline: A State Department cable signed by Rubio frames data sovereignty rules as obstacles to U.S. AI dominance
articles.body:
The Trump administration has instructed American diplomats worldwide to actively oppose foreign governments' efforts to regulate how U.S. technology companies store and process their citizens' data, according to an internal State Department cable reported by Reuters on February 25.

The directive, signed by Secretary of State Marco Rubio, characterizes data sovereignty laws as threats to artificial intelligence development and global commerce. The cable warns that such regulations would "disrupt global data flows, increase costs and cybersecurity risks, limit AI and cloud services, and expand government control in ways that can undermine civil liberties and enable censorship." Diplomats have been specifically ordered to "counter unnecessarily burdensome regulations, such as data localization mandates" — requirements that force companies to store user data within the borders of the country where it was collected.

Beyond lobbying against proposed rules, the cable tasks diplomats with monitoring and tracking legislative efforts worldwide that would advance data sovereignty frameworks. It also directs them to promote the Global Cross-Border Privacy Rules Forum, an international body that facilitates data transfers through voluntary privacy certifications rather than mandatory localization requirements. The U.S. State Department did not respond to requests for comment.

The directive lands amid a global wave of data regulation. The European Union has established itself as the most aggressive regulator through the General Data Protection Regulation, the Digital Services Act, and the AI Act — a suite of laws designed to limit how technology companies collect, exploit, and profit from user data. Other nations across Asia, Africa, and Latin America have increasingly pursued similar frameworks, motivated by concerns over digital sovereignty and the concentration of data in American corporate infrastructure.

The cable represents the clearest articulation yet of how the administration plans to leverage diplomatic channels to protect the competitive position of U.S. AI and cloud computing firms. Companies like Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and OpenAI depend on the free flow of international data to train AI models and deliver cloud services at scale. Data localization mandates can impose significant infrastructure costs and limit the datasets available for AI training. Critics, however, argue that the approach effectively asks foreign governments to subordinate their citizens' privacy interests to American commercial objectives — a framing that could complicate diplomatic relationships, particularly with allies in Europe who view robust data protection as a fundamental right.

articles.headline: Allianz Announces Up to €2.5 Billion Share Buyback Program
articles.subheadline: German insurance giant returns excess capital to shareholders as CEO Baete continues investor-friendly strategy
articles.body:
Allianz SE, Europe's largest insurer and parent company of bond management powerhouse Pacific Investment Management Co. (PIMCO), has unveiled plans to repurchase up to €2.5 billion of its own shares, signaling continued confidence in its financial position and a commitment to returning surplus capital to investors.

The buyback announcement, disclosed on Tuesday, represents the latest move by Chief Executive Officer Oliver Baete to channel excess cash back to shareholders. Allianz has pursued a consistent capital return strategy in recent years, using a combination of dividends and share repurchases to reward investors as the Munich-based company generates robust cash flows from its insurance and asset management operations.

The program underscores the strong capital position that major European insurers have maintained, even amid a shifting macroeconomic landscape. Allianz, which manages assets across its insurance divisions and through PIMCO — one of the world's largest fixed-income investors — has benefited from higher interest rates that have bolstered investment returns on its vast portfolio. The company's Solvency II ratio, a key measure of financial resilience for European insurers, has remained comfortably above regulatory requirements, giving management room to deploy capital toward shareholder returns.

Share buybacks have become an increasingly popular tool among Europe's blue-chip financial firms, with insurers and banks alike using repurchases to boost earnings per share and signal management's belief that their stock remains attractively valued. For Allianz, the €2.5 billion ceiling on the latest program represents a substantial commitment, reinforcing its position among the continent's most shareholder-friendly companies.

The announcement comes as European insurers broadly navigate a period of solid profitability, supported by disciplined underwriting and favorable investment conditions. Investors will be watching for further details on the timing and execution of the buyback, as well as any signals from Baete's leadership team about the company's broader capital allocation priorities heading deeper into 2026.

articles.headline: US Lends $26.5 Billion to Southern Co. for Power Projects
articles.subheadline: Trump administration closes massive financing package as data center boom strains grid capacity and affordability
articles.body:
The Trump administration finalized a $26.5 billion loan package for Southern Company power projects in Georgia and Alabama on Wednesday, marking one of the largest federal energy financing deals in recent memory as the nation grapples with surging electricity demand and growing concerns over affordability.

The loan, closed by the U.S. Department of Energy, is directed at bolstering power generation infrastructure operated by subsidiaries of Southern Company, one of the largest electric utilities in the United States. The deal comes at a moment when the American power grid faces unprecedented strain from the rapid proliferation of electricity-hungry data centers, driven largely by the artificial intelligence boom, as well as broader electrification trends across the economy.

Rising electricity costs have become a political flashpoint, with consumers and regulators alike raising alarms about the affordability of keeping the lights on. Data centers alone are projected to consume a rapidly growing share of U.S. electricity output in the coming years, and utilities across the Southeast — a region that has attracted significant investment from hyperscale cloud and AI operators — are scrambling to build out generation capacity to meet the demand. Southern Company, headquartered in Atlanta, serves approximately nine million customers across several southeastern states and has been at the center of these expansion efforts.

The scale of the federal financing underscores the administration's willingness to deploy substantial government capital to accelerate energy infrastructure buildout, even as debates continue over the appropriate mix of generation sources. Southern Company's portfolio includes nuclear, natural gas, and renewable assets, and the company has been a key player in the development of the Vogtle nuclear expansion in Georgia — a project that experienced significant cost overruns and delays before its final units came online.

The loan package is likely to face scrutiny from fiscal hawks and energy policy analysts who will examine whether the terms adequately protect taxpayers. But proponents argue that federal financing is essential to keeping pace with electricity demand that threatens to outstrip supply, potentially leading to reliability crises and further price increases for households and businesses across the region.

articles.headline: Blockfills CEO Steps Down as Crypto Lender Reels From $75M Loss
articles.subheadline: Chicago-based firm froze client withdrawals earlier this month and is now seeking a buyer amid broader market weakness
articles.body:
Nicholas Hammer, co-founder of institutional crypto lender Blockfills, has exited the chief executive role as the firm grapples with approximately $75 million in lending losses and a freeze on customer deposits and withdrawals that has persisted since February 11. Joseph Perry has been named interim CEO, according to the company's website.

The leadership shakeup at the Chicago-based firm, which processed more than $60 billion in trading volume in 2025 — a 28 percent increase year over year — underscores the fragility that still haunts crypto lending even as the industry matures. A person with direct knowledge of the situation told CoinDesk that some clients were encouraged to pull their assets from the platform before the withdrawal halt took effect, raising questions about whether all customers received equal treatment. Blockfills is now actively seeking a buyer, according to earlier reporting.

The episode carries uncomfortable echoes of the 2022 crypto winter, when Celsius, BlockFi, and Genesis each froze customer accounts as cascading losses tore through the sector. Blockfills serves roughly 2,000 institutional clients — hedge funds, asset managers, and mining operations among them — and counts Susquehanna Private Equity Investments, CME Ventures, and Nexo among its backers. The firm raised $37 million in a Series A round in January 2022.

Blockfills said in a February 11 statement that it was working with investors and clients to restore liquidity and that customers could still open and close positions in spot and derivatives markets. But as of publication, deposit and withdrawal capabilities remain suspended, leaving client funds in limbo. Hammer did not respond to requests for comment, and his LinkedIn profile still listed him as CEO as of February 25.

The turmoil arrives during a difficult stretch for digital assets more broadly. Bitcoin has remained below $70,000 following a sharp retreat from its late-2025 record high, while ether trades near $2,000, reflecting cautious sentiment across the market. The Blockfills situation will likely intensify calls for stronger risk management standards and regulatory oversight in institutional crypto lending — a segment that has repeatedly proved vulnerable to sudden market dislocations despite its growing scale.

articles.headline: Jamie Dimon Warns Rivals Are Repeating Pre-2008 'Dumb Things' in Credit
articles.subheadline: JPMorgan CEO's concerns about reckless lending echo financial crisis parallels as macro analysts back his assessment
articles.body:
JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon has issued a pointed warning that competitors are engaging in reckless behavior in the credit markets, drawing explicit parallels to the kind of risk-taking that precipitated the 2008 global financial crisis.

Dimon, who has led the largest U.S. bank since 2005 and navigated it through the last major financial meltdown, said rivals are doing "dumb things" in credit — a characterization that Bloomberg macro strategist Simon White described as well founded. The comments come at a time when credit spreads remain historically tight and leveraged lending has surged, conditions that some analysts view as signs of complacency among market participants chasing yield in an increasingly competitive landscape.

The JPMorgan chief's warnings carry particular weight given his track record. During the lead-up to the 2008 crisis, Dimon was among the few major bank CEOs who recognized early signs of excess in mortgage-related credit products. His bank emerged from that episode in a stronger competitive position than most peers, in part because of a more disciplined approach to risk. Now, with private credit markets having ballooned past $1.7 trillion and traditional banks competing aggressively for lending business, Dimon appears to see a familiar pattern forming — one where the pressure to maintain market share leads institutions to underwrite deals with deteriorating terms and insufficient safeguards.

White's analysis supports the thesis that current credit market dynamics warrant concern. When spreads compress to levels that no longer adequately compensate for default risk, and when covenant protections erode in loan agreements, the system becomes more fragile and vulnerable to sudden repricing. The danger is not necessarily an imminent repeat of 2008, but rather that accumulated risk-taking could amplify the next economic downturn whenever it arrives.

For investors and regulators alike, Dimon's remarks serve as a reminder that periods of apparent market calm often mask the buildup of systemic vulnerabilities. Whether his peers heed the warning — or dismiss it as competitive posturing — may not be clear until the next credit cycle turns.

articles.headline: UBS Wealth CIO: Investors Should Pivot From Coders to Builders
articles.subheadline: AI disruption threatens software stocks; miners, power producers, and industrials stand to benefit, top strategist says
articles.body:
Investors should rotate out of software-heavy technology stocks and into companies that operate in the physical world — miners, power producers, and industrial firms — as artificial intelligence increasingly threatens to disrupt digital business models, according to a top UBS Wealth Management strategist.

Ulrike Hoffmann-Burchardi, global head of equities and chief investment officer for the Americas at UBS Wealth Management, argued that AI's rapid advance poses a material risk to software-backed businesses, many of which have commanded premium valuations for years on the assumption of durable competitive moats. As AI tools grow more capable of writing code, automating workflows, and replicating software functionality, those moats may prove far shallower than investors have priced in.

The recommendation marks a notable shift in tone from one of the world's largest wealth managers. For much of the past decade, the prevailing institutional view favored asset-light technology companies over capital-intensive industries. Hoffmann-Burchardi's thesis flips that calculus: the very physicality of mining, energy generation, and industrial manufacturing — the difficulty of replicating a copper mine or a power plant with a large language model — becomes a source of durable value rather than a drag on returns.

The argument also dovetails with broader market trends. Surging demand for data centers and AI infrastructure has already driven a re-rating of power utilities and energy companies, while industrial firms supplying the physical backbone of the AI buildout have seen renewed investor interest. At the same time, several high-flying software names have faced valuation compression as the market reassesses which businesses AI will augment and which it will commoditize.

For allocators, the message from UBS is straightforward: in an era where code can be generated at near-zero marginal cost, scarcity resides in atoms, not bits. Whether the market fully embraces that rotation remains to be seen, but the strategic case for real-world builders over digital incumbents is gaining influential advocates.
